disease.name: Colonic Diverticulitis
disease.note: Inflammation of colonic diverticula, most commonly in the sigmoid colon in Western populations but right-sided in Asian populations. Hinchey classification guides management. In Kenya, true diverticulitis is less common than in the West due to higher fiber diets, but incidence is rising with dietary westernization. Key distinction: left-sided diverticulitis can mimic colorectal cancer — always follow up with colonoscopy after resolution.
pathophysiologyDetail: Diverticula form at four points of weakness in the colonic wall where the vasa recta penetrate the circular muscle layer, creating natural anatomic loci minoris resistentiae between the antimesenteric and mesenteric taeniae

Low dietary fiber reduces stool bulk, narrows colonic diameter, and increases intraluminal pressure per Laplace's law (pressure = tension / radius), forcing mucosa and submucosa through muscle wall defects
Colonic segmentation generates high-pressure zones up to 90 mmHg in the sigmoid, where the diameter is narrowest (3-4 cm) and intraluminal pressures are highest during muscular contraction
Elastin deposition and collagen cross-linking abnormalities weaken the colonic wall over time, with progressive accumulation of diverticula as age advances beyond the fifth decade
Fecalith or inspissated stool lodges in the narrow neck of a diverticulum (typically 2-5 mm), causing mechanical obstruction, stasis, and bacterial overgrowth within the sac

Obstruction of the diverticular orifice leads to mucosal ischemia from impaired microcirculation, triggering neutrophil infiltration and release of proteolytic enzymes that erode through the thin diverticular wall (typically only mucosa and attenuated muscularis mucosae)

A phlegmon forms when the omentum and adjacent mesentery wall off the micro-perforation — this represents the body's attempt to contain the inflammatory process and is the most common finding in uncomplicated diverticulitis

Pericolic abscess develops when purulent material collects in the pericolonic fat but remains contained by the mesentery and surrounding structures; abscess size (>4 cm) determines need for drainage versus medical therapy alone

Free perforation with generalized peritonitis occurs when the inflammatory process overwhelms containment mechanisms, spilling colonic contents into the peritoneal cavity — more common in immunosuppressed patients and NSAID users

Hinchey classification stages the anatomical severity: I (pericolic phlegmon or small abscess <4 cm), II (pelvic/intra-abdominal/retroperitoneal abscess >4 cm), III (generalized purulent peritonitis), IV (generalized fecal peritonitis with frank stool contamination)

Chronic or recurrent inflammation stimulates fibroblast proliferation and collagen deposition, leading to concentric fibrosis and stricture formation in the affected sigmoid segment over months to years

Fistulogenesis begins when the inflamed sigmoid adheres to an adjacent organ (bladder, vagina, small bowel, skin) and the inflammatory process erodes through both visceral walls, creating an epithelialized communication
Colovesical fistula is the most common type (65% of diverticular fistulas), presenting with pneumaturia, fecaluria, and recurrent polymicrobial UTIs — the triad is virtually diagnostic
Colovaginal fistulas occur preferentially in women who have undergone prior hysterectomy, as the uterus no longer separates the sigmoid from the vaginal cuff

Right-sided diverticulitis (cecum and ascending colon) constitutes 15-20% of cases in Asian populations, in contrast to <5% in Western populations; it is commonly misdiagnosed as appendicitis, leading to unnecessary appendectomy

Genetic predisposition involves mutations in connective tissue genes (COL3A1, COL5A1, ELN) and increased MMP activity, explaining the strong association with Ehlers-Danlos, Marfan, and Williams-Beuren syndromes
Immunosuppression from chronic corticosteroids, chemotherapy, or HIV/AIDS dramatically increases the risk of perforation, with odds ratios of 3-5 for complicated disease and worse outcomes

NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase-mediated prostaglandin synthesis, reducing mucosal blood flow and protective mucus secretion — this increases perforation risk 2-3 fold by impairing mucosal defense mechanisms

Cigarette smoking impairs colonic microvascular circulation, reduces tissue oxygenation, and dampens immune function, doubling the risk of complicated diverticulitis and recurrence after medical treatment

Visceral obesity increases intra-abdominal pressure (transmitted to the colonic wall), promotes systemic inflammation via adipokine release, and impairs immune surveillance — each 5-unit BMI increase raises complication risk by 20%

landmarkTrials.population: 623 patients with uncomplicated diverticulitis in Sweden and Iceland
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in recovery time, recurrence rate, or complication rate between groups. 97% recovered without antibiotics.
landmarkTrials.impact: Challenged the long-standing dogma that antibiotics are mandatory for all diverticulitis. Current guidelines now recommend selective antibiotic use for uncomplicated cases based on severity and comorbidities.

landmarkTrials.population: 528 patients with acute uncomplicated diverticulitis across 22 Dutch hospitals
landmarkTrials.intervention: IV antibiotics vs supportive care (no antibiotics)
landmarkTrials.outcome: No difference in time to recovery (12 vs 11 days, p=0.64). Recurrence at 12 months: 7.6% antibiotics vs 8.2% observation. Complication rates similar.
landmarkTrials.impact: Largest RCT confirming antibiotics are not necessary for most uncomplicated diverticulitis. Supportive care with analgesia and dietary modification is sufficient. Antibiotic stewardship implications are significant.

landmarkTrials.population: 83 patients with perforated diverticulitis (Hinchey III — purulent peritonitis)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic lavage vs open sigmoid resection (Hartmann procedure)
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in postoperative morbidity. Lavage group had shorter operative time, less stoma formation, and shorter hospital stay. Reoperation rate higher in lavage group (20% vs 8%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Introduced laparoscopic lavage as a potential alternative to Hartmann procedure for Hinchey III. Controversial — subsequent trials showed higher reoperation rates. Currently reserved for highly selected patients.

landmarkTrials.population: 90 patients with perforated diverticulitis across 34 centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic lavage vs sigmoidectomy (Hartmann or primary anastomosis)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Lavage group: 67% had ongoing peritonitis requiring reintervention vs 12% in resection group (p<0.001). Study stopped early due to safety concerns.
landmarkTrials.impact: Showed laparoscopic lavage is inferior to resection for Hinchey III peritonitis — higher reoperation rate without long-term benefit. Current guidelines do not recommend lavage as standard for purulent peritonitis.

landmarkTrials.population: 199 patients with suspected perforated diverticulitis across 21 Scandinavian centers
landmarkTrials.outcome: No difference in 90-day mortality. Lavage group had higher rates of intra-abdominal abscess (32% vs 13%) and reoperation (27% vs 10%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Further evidence against routine laparoscopic lavage for perforated diverticulitis. Resection remains standard of care for Hinchey III-IV disease. Lavage may have a limited role in select hemodynamically stable patients.

landmarkTrials.population: 109 patients with recurrent diverticulitis or persistent complaints after previous episode
landmarkTrials.intervention: Elective sigmoid resection vs conservative management
landmarkTrials.outcome: Surgery group: better quality of life at 6 months (p<0.001). 96% of conservative group had recurrence within 2 years. Stoma rate in surgical group: 18%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated elective sigmoid resection improves quality of life in patients with recurrent diverticulitis. Decision for surgery should be individualized based on frequency and severity of attacks.

landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 8 randomized trials comparing surgical vs medical management
landmarkTrials.intervention: Elective sigmoid resection vs conservative treatment
landmarkTrials.outcome: Surgery reduced recurrence (RR 0.15) but had 30-day complication rate of 15-25%. Patient-reported quality of life favored surgery at 1 year.
landmarkTrials.impact: Helps guide shared decision-making: younger patients with frequent attacks benefit more from surgery; older patients with comorbidities may prefer medical management despite higher recurrence risk.

evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard — diagnoses diverticulitis, grades severity (Hinchey), detects abscess and free air, and rules out alternative diagnoses. Must have IV contrast; oral contrast optional.
Valuable first-line in Kenya — no radiation, inexpensive, and widely available. Shows thickened colonic wall (>4 mm), pericolic fat stranding, abscess. Operator-dependent.
CRP >50 mg/L strongly suggests complicated diverticulitis. Serial CRP monitoring guides treatment response. Normal CRP with LLQ pain makes diverticulitis less likely.
Leukocytosis with left shift supports infection. A normal WBC does not exclude diverticulitis, especially in immunocompromised patients.
Essential 6-8 weeks after acute episode to exclude colorectal cancer (present in 1-2% of patients with diverticulitis). Also confirms extent of diverticulosis and assesses for stricture.
When colovesical fistula suspected — contrast in bladder followed by CT. Can directly demonstrate fistulous tract or air in bladder.
Rule out UTI as cause of LLQ pain. Pyuria or bacteriuria may indicate colovesical fistula if recurrent and polymicrobial.
Elevated in sepsis/peritonitis — guides urgency of surgical intervention. Normal lactate does not rule out perforation.
Useful dietary assessment tool. Type 1-2 (hard stools) suggests constipation and high intraluminal pressure contributing to diverticulitis.
Elevated in acute diverticulitis but cannot distinguish from other colitides. More useful during follow-up to confirm resolution of inflammation.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical Assessment — Suspect diverticulitis in any patient >40 years with acute LLQ pain, fever, and LLQ tenderness. Also consider in younger patients with similar presentation, especially those with risk factors (obesity, low-fiber diet, smoking).
Step 2: Vital Signs & Sepsis Screen — Fever, tachycardia, hypotension indicate complicated disease (abscess or perforation). Calculate qSOFA or SIRS criteria to assess severity and need for ICU.

Step 3: Laboratory Workup — CBC, CRP, lactate, blood cultures (if febrile). CRP >50 mg/L with leukocytosis strongly supports complicated diverticulitis. Urinalysis to exclude UTI and screen for colovesical fistula.

Step 4: Imaging — CT abdomen with IV contrast is the gold standard. If CT unavailable (common in rural Kenyan settings), abdominal ultrasound is a reasonable alternative, especially in slim patients. Plain X-ray has limited sensitivity but may show free air.

Step 5: CT-Based Severity Grading — Apply Hinchey classification. Key findings: colonic wall thickening (>4 mm), fat stranding (pericolic or diffuse), abscess (measure size and location), extraluminal air (loculated or free), and fluid collections.

Step 6: Differentiate from Colon Cancer — Focal wall thickening >5 cm length with pericolonic fat stranding favors diverticulitis. Short segment (<5 cm) with shouldered margins and no fat stranding favors cancer. Colonoscopy at 6-8 weeks is mandatory to confirm.

Step 7: Assess for Fistula — If the patient reports pneumaturia, fecaluria, recurrent UTIs, or vaginal discharge of feces: suspect colovesical or colovaginal fistula. CT cystogram or pelvic MRI confirms.

Step 8: Decision for Admission — Admit if: complicated disease (abscess >4 cm, free air, peritonitis), inability to tolerate oral intake, severe pain requiring IV analgesia, significant comorbidities, or failed outpatient management.

Step 9: Inpatient Management — NBM, IV fluids, IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). CT-guided drainage of abscess >4 cm. Monitor clinical response — falling CRP and fever resolution signal improvement.

Step 10: Surgical Decision — Emergency surgery (Hartmann or sigmoid resection + anastomosis with covering stoma) for Hinchey III-IV. Elective sigmoid resection after 2-3 episodes of uncomplicated or 1 episode of complicated disease. Colonoscopy mandatory before elective surgery to exclude cancer.

managementPearls.pearl: Uncomplicated diverticulitis (Hinchey I) no longer automatically requires antibiotics. The DIABOLO and AVOD trials demonstrated supportive care alone (bowel rest, analgesia) is sufficient for mild cases. Reserve antibiotics for: immunocompromised patients, significant comorbidities, failure to improve after 48 hours, or CRP persistently >100 mg/L.

Abscess >4 cm requires CT-guided percutaneous drainage in addition to IV antibiotics. Abscess <4 cm can often be managed with IV antibiotics alone. Drainage success rates exceed 85% and avoid emergency surgery in most patients.
managementPearls.evidence: WSES Guidelines 2022; Brandt et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2006

managementPearls.pearl: Colonoscopy is mandatory 6-8 weeks after every episode of diverticulitis to exclude colorectal cancer. Prevalence of colon cancer in patients with radiologic diverticulitis is 1-2% overall, rising to 4-5% in those with complicated disease. Never perform colonoscopy during the acute phase — perforation risk is unacceptably high.
managementPearls.evidence: Sámano et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2017

managementPearls.pearl: Elective sigmoid resection should be considered after 2-3 episodes of uncomplicated diverticulitis or one episode of complicated disease. The DIRECT trial showed significantly better quality of life in surgical patients. However, each patient should be counseled on the 15-25% complication rate and 18% stoma rate.

In emergency surgery for Hinchey III-IV, Hartmann procedure remains the safest option for most surgeons. Primary resection with anastomosis and covering loop ileostomy can be considered in hemodynamically stable, well-nourished patients without significant comorbidities.

Laparoscopic lavage alone for Hinchey III (purulent peritonitis) is no longer recommended based on LADIES and SCANDIV trials showing unacceptably high reoperation rates (67% and 27%). Reserve for moribund patients who cannot tolerate resection — as a bridge to definitive surgery.

In right-sided diverticulitis (commoner in Asian and African populations): management is similar to left-sided, but the disease is often misdiagnosed intraoperatively as perforated appendicitis. If cecal diverticulitis is found during appendectomy, perform diverticulectomy alone — NOT right hemicolectomy — unless malignancy is confirmed.
managementPearls.evidence: Park et al., World J Surg 2015

managementPearls.pearl: NSAIDs are contraindicated in acute diverticulitis — they increase perforation risk 2-3 fold by impairing mucosal prostaglandin synthesis and reducing mucosal blood flow. Use Paracetamol or opioids for pain control. This advice extends to chronic NSAID use in patients with known diverticulosis.

High-fiber diet reduces recurrence risk but must be introduced gradually after resolution. Target 25-35 g/day from fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. In Kenya, traditional high-fiber foods (githeri, ugali with vegetables, sukuma wiki, whole maize) should be encouraged — these were protective before dietary westernization.
managementPearls.evidence: Crowe et al., BMJ 2011; UK dietary guidelines

managementPearls.pearl: In patients presenting with LLQ pain and fever in Kenya, always consider sigmoid volvulus (common in East Africa), amebic colitis (especially with dysentery), and obstructed left colon cancer. CT is essential for differentiation — the management differs completely.

operativeNuances.nuance: During elective laparoscopic sigmoid resection for diverticulitis, the most difficult part is identifying the left ureter in a chronically inflamed field. The ureter is often tethered by fibrosis. Place a ureteric stent preoperatively if there has been prior pelvic surgery or significant inflammation. The 'medial-to-lateral' approach helps identify the ureter at the pelvic brim where it crosses the common iliac artery.
operativeNuances.evidence: Senagore et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2015

operativeNuances.nuance: The distal resection margin must be at the rectosigmoid junction (upper rectum) — leaving sigmoid colon behind leads to recurrent diverticulitis in 5-10% of patients. The proximal margin should be in soft, compliant descending colon to enable a tension-free anastomosis. The 'top of the rectal fold' is the landmark for the distal transection line.

In the Hartmann procedure for perforated diverticulitis, the rectal stump must be closed securely and placed deep in the pelvis below the peritoneal reflection. Stump blowout is a catastrophic complication. Close with a TA stapler and consider reinforcing with a slowly absorbable suture. Leave a pelvic drain to detect any stump leak.

When performing a colorectal anastomosis after sigmoid resection, ensure the proximal colon is well-vascularized by checking the marginal artery at the planned transection line — use a Doppler or check for active bleeding from the cut edge. An inadequately vascularized anastomosis is the leading preventable cause of anastomotic leak.
operativeNuances.evidence: Ho et al., J Gastrointest Surg 2020

operativeNuances.nuance: In the emergency setting with a severely inflamed, thickened, and friable sigmoid mesentery, the mesenteric vessels may be difficult to control. Use a medial-to-lateral approach with early identification and division of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at its origin. This 'proximal vascular control' reduces bleeding and allows safe medial dissection.

For colovesical fistula repair: resect the sigmoid colon, separate the bladder from the colon, and primarily repair the bladder in two layers with absorbable suture. The bladder defect does NOT require omental interposition in most cases. A postoperative cystogram is not routinely needed — continue urethral catheter for 7-10 days.
operativeNuances.evidence: Melchior et al., Int J Colorectal Dis 2015

operativeNuances.nuance: In chronic diverticulitis with a fixed, inflamed inflammatory mass adherent to the pelvic sidewall, a 'difficult pelvis' scenario: use the 'top-down' approach (mobilize from the descending colon distally) rather than starting at the pelvic brim. Consider a ureteric stent, and have a low threshold to convert to open if laparoscopic planes are unclear.

prognosis.naturalHistory: 80-85% of patients with acute diverticulitis respond to medical therapy alone. Among those with uncomplicated disease, recurrence risk is 20-30% after the first episode, 30-40% after a second episode, and 50% after a third episode. Only 5-10% of patients with diverticulosis ever develop diverticulitis in their lifetime. Complicated disease recurs more frequently and earlier.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: Overall recurrence after medically managed diverticulitis: 20-30% at 5 years, 35-45% at 10 years. After elective sigmoid resection: recurrence rate 1-5% (can occur if sigmoid was not resected to the rectosigmoid junction). Recurrent attacks tend to be less severe than the initial episode.
prognosis.cancerRisk: The risk of colorectal cancer in patients with radiologically confirmed diverticulitis is 1-2% — higher than the general population. This is likely due to misdiagnosis (cancer masquerading as diverticulitis) rather than true malignant transformation. Colonoscopy at 6-8 weeks after resolution is mandatory to exclude malignancy.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Quality of life is significantly impaired during acute episodes and in patients with frequent recurrences. Chronic abdominal pain and bowel habit changes persist in 10-20% of patients after resolution. The DIRECT trial demonstrated that elective sigmoid resection significantly improves quality of life compared to continued medical management in patients with recurrent disease.
prognosis.mortality: Mortality for uncomplicated diverticulitis is <1%. For complicated disease: Hinchey III (purulent peritonitis) mortality 5-10%, Hinchey IV (fecal peritonitis) mortality 15-30%. Emergency surgery carries significantly higher mortality (15-25%) than elective resection (0.5-1.5%). Mortality is highest in immunocompromised patients, those with significant comorbidities, and elderly patients >80 years.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: immunosuppression (highest risk), NSAID use, smoking (doubles recurrence risk), obesity (BMI >30), age >80 years, and presence of comorbidities (diabetes, CKD, COPD). Protective factors: high-fiber diet (25-35 g/day), regular exercise, weight control, smoking cessation, and avoidance of NSAIDs. A diet rich in traditional Kenyan fiber sources (githeri, legumes, whole grains, sukuma wiki) is protective.

presentingComplaints: Persistent pain in the left lower abdomen that started gradually
Change in bowel habit — either constipation or diarrhea
Painful urination if the inflammation is near the bladder

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Generalized peritonitis/sepsis/Hinchey III-IV -> SEVERE; localized phlegmon/abscess I-II -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did the pain start and where exactly do you feel it?
Is the pain constant or cramping? Does it move?
Do you have a fever? Have you taken your temperature?
Have you had a similar episode before?
Have you ever been told you have diverticulosis (pouches in the colon)?
Have you had any recent change in bowel habit — constipation or diarrhea?
Do you have any pain when passing urine?
Have you noticed any blood in your stool?
What medications do you take regularly (especially NSAIDs like Ibuprofen, Diclofenac)?
Do you smoke or use any tobacco products?
What is your typical diet? How much fiber do you eat?
Have you ever had a colonoscopy?
Do you have any chronic illnesses like diabetes or kidney disease?
Are you on any steroids or immunosuppressant medications?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Have you had nausea or vomiting?
Do you have a family history of diverticular disease or colon cancer?
Have you tried any home or traditional remedies for this pain?
Does the pain get worse when you eat?
Is there anyone in your family with similar symptoms?
Do you use traditional or herbal remedies for abdominal pain? Many patients in Kenya use herbs like mchunga or aloe vera — have you tried anything?
What is your typical daily diet? How often do you eat foods like ugali, githeri, sukuma wiki, or chapati?
Have you had any recent weight loss or change in appetite?
Do you have any chronic conditions like diabetes, hypertension, or HIV?
Women: could you be pregnant? Have you had any gynecological problems recently?

examination_findings.finding: Left lower quadrant tenderness — localized and well-demarcated
Guarding or rigidity if peritonitis developing
Palpable mass in left lower quadrant — phlegmon or abscess
Fever and tachycardia — present in most acute cases
Abdominal distension if ileus or obstruction
Decreased bowel sounds from localized ileus
Digital rectal exam — may reveal tender mass or pelvic collection
Signs of dehydration (dry mucous membranes, reduced turgor)
Abdominal wall erythema in advanced cases with abscess
Cullen's sign (periumbilical bruising) in severe retroperitoneal bleeding
Right lower quadrant tenderness — consider right-sided diverticulitis, more common in Asian and African populations
Abdominal wall cellulitis or erythema over the inflamed segment in advanced cases
Psoas sign — pain on hip extension indicates retroperitoneal inflammation near the psoas muscle
Obturator sign — pain on internal rotation of flexed hip suggests pelvic inflammation or abscess

bedside_scores.name: Hinchey Classification (Modified)

components.name: Hinchey I — Pericolic phlegmon or small abscess (<4cm)
Hinchey II — Pelvic, intra-abdominal, or retroperitoneal abscess (>4cm)

bedside_scores.interpretation: I: medical management ± oral antibiotics; II: percutaneous drainage + IV antibiotics; III-IV: emergency surgery

subtypes.name: Uncomplicated (Hinchey I)
subtypes.management: Outpatient management possible. Oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin-Clavulanic acid 875/125mg BD or Metronidazole + Ciprofloxacin). Bowel rest, liquid diet. Analgesia (avoid NSAIDs). Colonoscopy in 6-8 weeks after resolution.

subtypes.name: Complicated (Hinchey II-IV)
subtypes.management: Hospital admission. IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). CT-guided percutaneous drainage of abscess >4cm. Emergency surgery (Hartmann procedure or primary resection with anastomosis + covering stoma) if generalized peritonitis or failed drainage.

subtypes.name: Right-Sided (Cecal/Ascending) Diverticulitis
subtypes.management: If diagnosed preoperatively: same as left-sided — antibiotics and bowel rest. If found incidentally during appendectomy: perform diverticulectomy alone. Avoid right hemicolectomy unless malignancy cannot be excluded. Right hemicolectomy reserved for: suspected cancer, massive inflammation, or complications.

subtypes.name: Chronic Diverticulitis with Stricture
subtypes.management: Elective sigmoid resection after full evaluation. Colonoscopy with biopsy mandatory to exclude malignancy (stricturing colon cancer can mimic diverticular stricture). Endoscopic balloon dilation is temporary — surgery is definitive. Laparoscopic sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis.

bedside.name: Vital signs — temperature, pulse, BP, respiratory rate
Digital rectal examination
bedside.indication: assess for pelvic tenderness or mass, blood on glove

laboratory.name: Urinalysis ± culture
laboratory.indication: rule out UTI, assess for colovesical fistula

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: gold standard — assess extent, abscess, perforation, alternative diagnoses

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: alternative in young/pregnant patients — can show thickened bowel, abscess

imaging.name: Colonoscopy
imaging.indication: 6-8 weeks after resolution to exclude malignancy and confirm diverticulosis

interpretation_rules.ct_colonic_wall_thickening: Focal wall thickening at the site of diverticulitis with fat stranding is diagnostic. Length of involvement >5cm and pericolonic fat stranding differentiate from malignancy.
interpretation_rules.hinchey_ct_classification: Hinchey I: small abscess <4cm or phlegmon. Hinchey II: abscess >4cm. Hinchey III: pus in peritoneal cavity. Hinchey IV: stool in peritoneal cavity.
interpretation_rules.abscess_size_indication: Abscess <4cm can be treated with antibiotics alone. Abscess >4cm requires CT-guided percutaneous drainage in addition to IV antibiotics.
interpretation_rules.colonoscopy_timing: NEVER perform colonoscopy during acute diverticulitis — risk of perforation. Wait 6-8 weeks after resolution.
interpretation_rules.elective_surgery_indications: After 2-3 episodes of uncomplicated diverticulitis, or one episode of complicated diverticulitis, consider elective sigmoid resection.

management.initial: Assess severity (CT-based Hinchey classification). IV fluids resuscitation. NBM if severe. Analgesia (avoid NSAIDs — increased perforation risk). IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. Admit if complicated or unable to tolerate oral intake.
management.definitive: Uncomplicated (Hinchey I): oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin-Clavulanic acid or Metronidazole + Ciprofloxacin) for 7-10 days. Liquid diet advancing as tolerated. Outpatient management possible. Complicated (Hinchey II): IV antibiotics + percutaneous drainage of abscess >4cm. Hinchey III-IV: emergency laparotomy. Hartmann procedure for feculent peritonitis. Primary resection with anastomosis and covering ileostomy for purulent peritonitis. Laparoscopic lavage (DILALA trial) — controversial, not for generalized peritonitis. Elective sigmoid resection after 2-3 episodes of uncomplicated or 1 episode of complicated diverticulitis.
management.postop: ICU for peritonitis/sepsis. Continue IV antibiotics. Monitor for ongoing sepsis. Early mobilization. VTE prophylaxis. Wound care. Diet advancement as tolerated. Plan for stoma reversal in 3-6 months if Hartmann. Colonoscopy in 6-8 weeks to exclude malignancy.

pathways.criteria: localized pain, mild fever, no peritonitis, small abscess <4cm or phlegmon only
pathways.treatment: Outpatient or short stay. Oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin-Clavulanic or Metronidazole + Ciprofloxacin) for 7-10 days. Liquid diet. Analgesia. Colonoscopy in 6-8 weeks.

pathways.criteria: abscess >4cm, pelvic/intra-abdominal collection, no generalized peritonitis
pathways.treatment: Admit. IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam). CT-guided percutaneous drainage of abscess. Monitor clinical response. Interval elective sigmoid resection.

pathways.criteria: purulent or fecal peritonitis, systemic toxicity, septic shock
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. IV resuscitation. Hartmann procedure (preferred for contamination). Sigmoid resection + primary anastomosis with covering ileostomy (selected cases). Peritoneal lavage. ICU. Long-term stoma reversal planning.

operative_templates.indication: Recurrent diverticulitis (2-3 episodes) or after complicated episode, with resolution of acute inflammation
operative_templates.incision: 4-5 port laparoscopic technique or midline laparotomy
steps: Patient in Lloyd-Davies position
Diagnostic laparoscopy — assess for adhesions, inflammation
Mobilize sigmoid colon — incise white line of Toldt
Identify and protect left ureter and gonadal vessels
Identify superior hemorrhoidal vessels and divide
Mobilize descending colon to ensure tension-free anastomosis
Divide colon at rectosigmoid junction (upper rectum)
Divide proximal margin at descending-sigmoid junction
Extract specimen via Pfannenstiel incision
Create colorectal anastomosis using EEA stapler
Air leak test
Close mesenteric defect
Place drain if indicated

operative_templates.indication: Generalized peritonitis from perforated diverticulitis (Hinchey III-IV)
steps: Midline laparotomy, immediate four-quadrant exploration
Copious peritoneal lavage with warm saline
Identify the perforated sigmoid segment
Mobilize sigmoid colon, identify ureters
Divide mesentery and ligate vessels
Divide sigmoid/rectum at the peritoneal reflection
Oversew or staple rectal stump — leave in pelvis
Bring descending colon out as end colostomy (left iliac fossa)
Further peritoneal lavage until clear
Place pelvic drain(s)
Close abdomen with mass closure (or VAC if contaminated)
Apply colostomy bag

dischargeCriteria: Patient educated on warning signs and high-fiber diet

patient_education.explanation: Diverticulitis occurs when small pouches (diverticula) in the wall of the colon become inflamed or infected. It most commonly affects the left side of the colon (sigmoid). In Kenya, traditional high-fiber diets protect against diverticular disease, but with more people eating processed foods, we are seeing more cases. Mild cases can be treated with antibiotics and diet. Severe cases with perforation or abscess may require drainage or emergency surgery.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if you have: worsening abdominal pain, fever that does not improve with antibiotics, vomiting, inability to eat or drink, increasing abdominal distension, or any bleeding from the back passage.
patient_education.activity: Rest during the acute episode. After recovery, regular physical activity helps prevent recurrence. Avoid heavy lifting for 2 weeks after surgery. After laparoscopic surgery, return to normal activity in 1-2 weeks; after open surgery, 4-6 weeks.
patient_education.diet: During acute episode: clear liquids only (water, clear soup, tea with sugar) for 2-3 days, then advance to low-fiber foods (white rice, white bread, bananas, cooked vegetables). After resolution: transition to high-fiber diet — fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and legumes. Eat at least 25-35g of fiber daily. Drink 8 glasses of water. Avoid nuts, seeds, popcorn, and maize seeds (traditional advice — recent evidence questions this restriction).
patient_education.medication: Take all prescribed antibiotics as directed — finish the full course even if you feel better. Avoid NSAIDs (Ibuprofen, Diclofenac) as they increase the risk of perforation. Use Paracetamol for pain instead. If you develop diarrhea during antibiotics, this may be a side effect.
patient_education.followUp: You MUST have a colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after the episode resolves to check for other causes such as colon cancer. If you had an abscess drained, a repeat CT scan may be needed to confirm resolution. If you have had 2 or more episodes, discuss elective sigmoid resection with your surgeon to prevent future attacks.

followup.before_discharge: Confirm afebrile and tolerating diet. Ensure outpatient antibiotic supply. Arrange colonoscopy appointment (6-8 weeks). Educate on dietary progression.

after_discharge.1_week: Clinical review — ensure symptoms resolving. Wound check if surgery. Review blood work.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Dietary advancement counseling. Confirm colonoscopy scheduled. Discuss elective surgery if recurrent episodes.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Colonoscopy performed. Review results. Discuss long-term prevention (high-fiber diet, lifestyle). Consider elective sigmoid resection if indicated.
after_discharge.3_months: Final follow-up if asymptomatic. Discharge to primary care for long-term constipation/diet management. Hernia surveillance if surgery performed.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: severe abdominal pain recurrence, fever not responding to antibiotics, vomiting, inability to eat, abdominal distension, heavy bleeding per rectum, or wound discharge/dehiscence if post-operative.
